system_prompt:
You are writing the "statement of outputs" — a human-readable narrative that
synthesizes everything the book teaches into a coherent description of the master/work's method.

The user will give you the book-level distillation, the chapter summaries, and
the structured systems-draft JSON you just derived. Write the statement.

Structure:

  # <Work Title> — Statement of Outputs

  ## Overview
  (2-3 paragraphs on what the book is and what it teaches, citing the
  book-level distillation.)

  ## Systems
  For each system in the systems-draft, one ## section. For each:
    - one paragraph describing the system in plain language
    - the system's members listed
    - the traversal rules and modification rules listed with their
      engine_payload kinds shown in code formatting
    - citations back to the relevant chapter summaries that backed each
      claim (e.g. "(Chapter 2 summary; ch02.md quotes)")

  ## Pending Work
  List every `_pending:<kebab>` engine_payload.kind value with what it
  signals. If there are none, omit this section.

  ## Provenance Notes
  Brief: what's drawn from what. Note any chapters that didn't yield a
  system and why.

This is the human-facing artifact. Be precise, cite, and don't invent
content outside the summaries and quote files.

# Book-level distillation — jazz-standards-playbook

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

This book teaches jazz improvisation through sequential study of ten carefully chosen jazz standards, using each tune as a vehicle for introducing and reinforcing a cumulative set of harmonic, melodic, and practice principles. The pedagogical architecture is explicitly ear-first: Chapter 1 establishes the LIST process (Listen, Internalize, Sing, Transfer) as the foundational method, positioning sheet music as a verification tool rather than a primary source, and centering effortful aural retrieval—singing melodies, bass lines, and chord roots before touching the instrument—as the engine of durable musical memory. Every subsequent chapter applies this orientation, grounding new harmonic concepts in specific tunes before abstracting them into transferable principles.

The book's harmonic vocabulary builds systematically across the chapters. The Major and Minor Diatonic Series of 7th Chords, introduced in Chapter 2 through Autumn Leaves, provides the foundational framework for reading scale degrees and chord qualities. The ii-V-I progression is declared the cornerstone of jazz harmony in that same chapter and reappears in virtually every subsequent analysis. Guide tones—the 3rds and 7ths of chords—are established early as the primary melodic spine of improvisation and the defining intervals for chord quality, and voice leading (smooth stepwise motion of guide tones across chord changes) is presented as the governing principle for melodic connection. Secondary dominants and tonicization appear in Chapter 4 (All of Me), where the master introduces the relational principle that a chord's function depends on context—the chords before and after it—not on its intrinsic quality in isolation. Chapters 5 through 9 elaborate this harmonic system through target chords, tritone substitution, chromatic passing chords, diminished 7th chords as rootless dominant b9 substitutes, and the Whole-Half diminished scale. Chapter 9 (Have You Met Miss Jones) introduces the cycle of major thirds as an analytical device for navigating rapid modulations, while Chapter 10 (All the Things You Are) consolidates cycling fourths as a foundational harmonic movement throughout the jazz repertoire.

A parallel strand of modal and chromatic theory runs through the later chapters. Chapter 6 introduces the Mixolydian scale—defined as a major scale with a flatted 7th (1-2-3-4-5-6-b7)—and reframes scales not as linear exercises but as pitch collections: harmonic resource pools from which melodic choices are drawn over chord changes. Chapter 7 (So What) introduces modal harmony as a distinct system from functional harmony, teaching Dorian mode (W-H-W-W-W-H-W) and the principle that guide tones are unnecessary in modal contexts. The master models Miles Davis's sparse, space-conscious approach to modal playing as the idiomatic application of modal knowledge, warning against mechanical scale-running in favor of selective note choice. Throughout both functional and modal contexts, the book prescribes taking all licks and patterns through all 12 keys as the concrete internalization method.

The book establishes several named analytical tools and reductive labels as characteristic moves. The backdoor dominant (bVII7 substituting for V7 by whole-step approach) and its extension, the backdoor ii-V, appear in Chapters 6 and 11. The hybrid ii-V-I—mixing minor ii-V from a minor key while resolving to a major chord—is introduced in Chapter 11 (Stella by Starlight) as an expressive harmonic surprise. The cycle-of-fourths functions as both an analytical tool for identifying substitution relationships and a compositional pattern observable across multiple standards. The minor-third key-center shift is identified in Chapter 8 as a recurring compositional pattern. The master's characteristic move throughout is to attach memorable labels to complex harmonic phenomena—backdoor, tritone substitution, tonicization—making analytical recognition faster and improvisation decisions more immediate.

Improvisation method is taught through a layered set of concrete practice protocols rather than abstract principles. Chapter 3's three-chorus melody exercise scaffolds from playing melody straight, to embellishing it as a guideline, to freely improvising while referencing melodic fragments—making the tune's identity audible even during extended departures. Chapter 8 prescribes playing as few notes as possible, sometimes one note over an entire sequence, to isolate chord-tone hearing. Chapter 11 introduces quarter-note solo exercises to separate harmonic navigation from rhythmic complexity, and note-mapping to conceptualize available pitch choices. Chapter 5 introduces the contrafact—a new melody composed over an existing song's chord changes—as a compositional practice tool, with two forms: a simple singable melody outlining chord changes and a busier bebop-style head with deliberate structural repetition. Chapter 10 consolidates six core improvisation tools: guide tones, melody, chord tones, contrafacts, motifs, and transcribed licks, adding composed solos as a method for slowing improvisation down to develop jazz language.

The master's characteristic aesthetic principles, stated explicitly across multiple chapters, include: strong melody licenses harmonic freedom including outside playing, provided lines resolve with intention (Chapter 3); improvisation is composition accelerated (Chapter 5); space and melodic breathing are hallmarks of strong improvisation (Chapter 5); simplicity over crowding governs harmonic navigation in dense passages (Chapter 8); and the defining-notes question—what are the defining notes of each chord, and which intervals (especially 3rds and 7ths) must change?—serves as the analytical tool for any harmonically confusing passage (Chapter 8). The book's closing chapter frames each standard not as an isolated piece but as an exemplar of harmonic principles transferable across hundreds of other tunes, positioning the standards themselves as vehicles—platforms for improvisation exploration—and characterizing music as a lifetime study without a final arrival point, reorienting the student from mastery toward perpetual exploration.

# Chapter 1 — Before We Start: The Pro Guide to Learning Jazz Standards

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 establishes ear-first learning as the foundational pedagogical method for jazz standards, introducing the LIST process (Listen, Internalize, Sing, Transfer) as the book's central framework for aural acquisition. The chapter teaches that jazz musicians must prioritize listening to multiple recordings, practicing intentional undivided attention, singing melodies and bass lines as proof of internalization, and then transferring that knowledge to the instrument through melody first, followed by chord learning via root motion and quality identification. Key techniques include learning vocal versions and lyrics to deepen melodic memory, using Frank Sinatra as a reference for straight melody renditions, and isolating bass notes to train harmonic ear training. The master's characteristic move is reframing sheet music not as a primary learning tool but as a checking mechanism, positioning effortful retrieval through repetition—rather than notation—as the engine of lasting musical retention and real-time improvisational responsiveness.

# Chapter 2 — Jazz Standard Study #1: Autumn Leaves

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 introduces the harmonic and formal foundations for analyzing and improvising over Autumn Leaves, a jazz standard in AABA form. The chapter establishes the Major and Minor Diatonic Series of 7th Chords as the primary framework for reading scale degrees and chord qualities, clarifies the convention of "concert keys" as the standard performance pitches for jazz standards, and defines the relative key relationship that governs Autumn Leaves' harmonic structure. The master's characteristic move throughout is the emphasis on practical jazz conventions—such as the modal mixture of minor IV to dominant seventh and the pervasive ii-V-I progression—over strict classical theory. The chapter introduces three load-bearing terms: guide tones (the 3rds and 7ths of chords, which define quality and serve as the melodic spine of improvisation), voice leading (the smooth stepwise motion of these tones from chord to chord), and the ii-V-I progression, declared the "cornerstone" of jazz harmony that students must recognize and improvise over immediately. The chapter concludes with a concrete practice method: playing guide tones isolated in half or quarter notes through the entire song form.

# Chapter 3 — Jazz Standard Study #2: Blue Bossa

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches the foundational elements of interpreting and improvising over the jazz standard Blue Bossa, beginning with its signature rhythmic characteristic: straight eighth notes rather than swung feel. The chapter establishes Blue Bossa's 16-bar form as structurally essential knowledge and introduces dual harmonic frameworks—the iv-VII relationship to C minor and the ii-V of relative major—for understanding bars 3-4, alongside the explicit mechanism of key center change to Db major via ii-V-I progression. A key generative principle emerges: strong melody licenses harmonic freedom, even "outside" playing, provided the improvised line resolves with intention. The chapter's characteristic pedagogical move is the three-chorus melody exercise, which scaffolds improvisation development from playing the melody straight, to embellishing it as a guideline, to freely improvising while periodically referencing melodic fragments—a concrete practice protocol that makes the tune's identity audible even during extended departures from the written melody.

# Chapter 4 — Jazz Standard Study #3: All of Me

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches harmonic analysis of the jazz standard "All of Me" through the lens of chord function and voice leading. The central principle is that a chord's function depends on its harmonic context—both the chords before and after it—establishing that analysis is relational rather than absolute. Key concepts introduced include secondary dominants and tonicization (when a dominant 7 chord acts as V of a non-tonic diatonic chord), explicit formulas for major 7, dominant 7, minor 7, half-diminished, and diminished 7 chords, and the chromatic approach as a named technique for non-structural melodic movement. The chapter reinforces that chord tones (particularly guide tones: 3rds and 7ths) rather than scales are the primary material for improvisation, and establishes a voice-leading rule for chord tone arpeggios: they always move in order (Root-3-5-7 or any rotation), with smooth connection across chord changes determined by proximity to the next chord. Characteristic moves include analyzing the IV-to-iv minor pivot as tonicization into C minor and introducing the cycle-of-fourths as an analytical tool for substitution chords, while cautioning against major intervals when improvising over iv minor chords.

# Chapter 5 — Jazz Standard Study #4: It Could Happen to You

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 teaches harmonic analysis and improvisation through the jazz standard "It Could Happen to You," establishing target chords as the analytical lens for understanding chord progressions—where a target chord is the arrival point that preceding chords resolve toward, and harmonic analysis must always interpret chords functionally in relation to their resolution rather than in isolation. The chapter introduces ii-V progressions as secondary dominant expansions, dual analytical readings such as interpreting the same chord pair as both a ii-V of ii and as iii(b5)-VI7, and the minor iv chord substitution. A contrafact—a new melody composed over an existing song's chord changes—is presented as a compositional practice tool for improvisation, with two approaches: a simple, singable melody that outlines chord changes and expresses thematic content, and a busier bebop-style head that maintains repeated sections, rhythmic patterns, and deliberate structure despite its complexity. The master's characteristic moves visible here include the principle that improvisation is composition accelerated, the emphasis on space and melodic breathing, and the use of thematic repetition with variation as hallmarks of strong improvisation.

# Chapter 6 — Jazz Standard Study #5: Sweet Georgia Brown

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 applies modal and chromatic theory to the jazz standard Sweet Georgia Brown, using its unusual VI7 opener and extended dominant cycles to teach harmonic conversion and improvisational navigation. The chapter establishes that diatonic minor chords can permissibly convert to dominant 7ths, then applies this rule to analyze bars 1–13 as a cycle of resolving dominants, the chromatic turnaround section as descending dominants targeting VI7, and the "backdoor" I7-VI7-ii-V-I device as a memorable reductive label for complex harmonic motion. The master introduces the Mixolydian scale—the fifth mode, constructed as a major scale with a flatted 7th (formula: 1-2-3-4-5-6-b7)—and reframes scales not as linear drills but as "pitch collections," harmonic resource pools that guide melodic choice over chord changes. Chord-tone pattern exercises and the practice mandate to take all licks through 12 keys embed the concrete internalization method central to the book's improvisational pedagogy.

# Chapter 7 — Jazz Standard Study #6: So What

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 introduces modal harmony through the jazz standard "So What," a foundational departure from the functional harmony that dominates earlier standards in the book. The chapter establishes that modal harmony operates on modes derived from a parent major scale—specifically, D Dorian (built from C major) with a brief modulation to Eb Dorian (from Db major)—and teaches students that guide tones are unnecessary in modal contexts. The core techniques presented include the Dorian mode formula (W-H-W-W-W-H-W), voice-leading principles for modal arpeggios that connect via the nearest chord tone rather than always starting on the root, and modal pattern exercises in thirds that link adjacent modes through half- and whole-step intervals. Rather than mechanically running scales, students learn to use modes as note choices within improvisation. The chapter distills Miles Davis's characteristic approach to this tune: sparse, space-conscious playing that stays primarily within the Dorian mode while occasionally employing chromatic approach notes, modeling how to apply modal knowledge idiomatically rather than literally.

# Chapter 8 — Jazz Standard Study #7: On Green Dolphin Street

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 applies harmonic substitution and voice-leading principles to the jazz standard On Green Dolphin Street, a tune built around the signature move of replacing the I chord with its i minor relative in bar 3—a substitution pattern the book cross-references from earlier chapters. The chapter introduces tritone substitution (a chord replaced by another with a root a tritone away) and chromatic passing chords, then teaches the minimal-change voice-leading principle: when improvising or arranging over confusing harmonic changes, keep it simple by asking "what are the defining notes of each chord?" and adjusting only the intervals (especially 3rds and 7ths) that carry the chord quality. Key load-bearing concepts include the minor-third key-center shift as a recurring compositional pattern, the defining-notes question as an analytical tool for any harmonically dense passage, motif (a short rhythmic or melodic passage repeated throughout a composition), chromatic passing tones (non-diatonic half-step connectors), and the explicit permission to use extensions (9ths, 11ths, 13ths) over 7th chords. The chapter governs improvisation strategy with the principle of simplicity over crowding, and offers a concrete practice technique—playing as few notes as possible, sometimes just one note over an entire sequence—to isolate chord-tone hearing in the practice room.

# Chapter 9 — Jazz Standard Study #8: Have You Met Miss Jones

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 applies harmonic substitution and voice-leading techniques to the jazz standard "Have You Met Miss Jones," focusing on how diminished 7 chords function both as rootless dominant b9 substitutes and as chromatic passing chords. The chapter establishes that F#dim7 shares all pitch classes with D7(b9) except the root, making this substitution relationship analytically transparent and aurally convincing. A central architectural feature is the bridge's key centers, which move in a cycle of major thirds (Bb–Gb–D–Ab), a structure the chapter reframes as stacked ii–V–I progressions in different momentary key centers to make the rapid modulation navigable. For improvisation over diminished chords, the chapter teaches students to treat the passing dim7 as a dominant b9 and target its chord tones, and introduces the Whole-Half diminished scale (W–H–W–H–W–H–W) as the theoretical basis for note choice. The melodic techniques include stepwise connection at bar boundaries when moving between key centers, though the chapter explicitly validates phrase separation as an equally valid alternative, reflecting the author's characteristic balance between principle and musical flexibility.

# Chapter 10 — Jazz Standard Study #9: All the Things You Are

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 analyzes "All the Things You Are," a 36-bar AABA jazz standard in Ab major that serves as an intensive study of cycling 4ths—a foundational harmonic movement in jazz. The chapter teaches how the tune opens with a vi chord that initiates a diatonic cycle of 4ths, how key transpositions repeat the first eight bars in new centers (with the V chord acting as the "secret weapon" for resolving to new keys), and how passing diminished chords function chromatically to connect chord changes. Jerome Kern's harmonic choices in bars 29-32 deliberately depart from pure cycle-of-4ths motion, offering players an alternative conceptual framework for non-diatonic passages. Beyond harmonic analysis, the chapter consolidates six core improvisation tools—guide tones, melody, chord tones, contrafacts, motifs, and transcribed licks—and emphasizes composed solos as a deliberate practice method to slow improvisation down and develop jazz language, whether notated or memorized directly on the instrument.

# Chapter 11 — Jazz Standard Study #10: Stella by Starlight

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 introduces jazz harmonic devices through analysis of Stella by Starlight, emphasizing that Real Book chord changes—not the original film version—form the standard for group performance. The chapter's analytical foundation uses color-coding to map secondary tonal centers back to a parent key, with the song's defining characteristic being a series of unresolved ii-V progressions in the opening that evade rather than establish a key center. Key concepts include the backdoor dominant (a bVII7 substituting for V7 by whole-step approach) and its extended form, the backdoor ii-V, which allow multiple harmonic interpretations of single chords; the hybrid ii-V-I, which mixes minor ii-V from a minor key while resolving to a major chord, creating an expressive surprise; and a closing cycle-of-4ths built from minor ii-Vs rather than dominant ii-Vs. The chapter prescribes practical improvisation tools: note-mapping to conceptualize available pitch choices, quarter-note solo exercises to isolate harmonic navigation from rhythm, and the dual-interpretation mindset that reads a single chord through multiple functional lenses simultaneously.

# Chapter 12 — Wrapping Things Up

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch12.md`._

Chapter 12 frames jazz standards as the essential vehicles for learning improvisation, establishing that deep study of their harmonic and rhythmic structures builds transferable skills across the entire jazz repertoire. The chapter's core pedagogical move is to position the standards selected throughout the book not as isolated pieces but as exemplars of harmonic principles that apply to hundreds of others, making each standard a gateway to broader understanding. The master's characteristic perspective emerges in the insistence that music is a lifetime study with no final arrival point—a rule-of-practice that reorients the student's relationship to the material beyond mastery toward perpetual exploration. The load-bearing terminology centers on "vehicles" (standards as platforms for improvisation exploration) and the implicit principle that harmonic and rhythmic transferability is the measure of pedagogical value.

          "name": "Relational Chord Function Analysis",
          "summary": "Always interpret a chord's function by examining both the chord before and after it; harmonic analysis is contextual, not absolute.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "FamilyCoherence"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "chord function depends on context",
              "quote_excerpt": "To understand the function of a chord, you must look at both the chord that comes before it and after it",
              "page": 61
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 5,
              "topic": "harmony analysis approach",
              "quote_excerpt": "the most important thing to pay attention to is where the chords are going",
              "page": 79
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "minor-to-dominant-conversion",
          "name": "Diatonic Minor to Dominant 7 Conversion",
          "summary": "Any diatonic minor chord may be converted to a dominant 7 chord; this is a standard jazz harmonic practice that expands the pool of resolving dominants.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SubstitutionExpand"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 2,
              "topic": "Minor IV chord to dominant",
              "quote_excerpt": "jazz musicians are constantly turning minor chords into dominant 7 chords",
              "page": 33
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 6,
              "topic": "diatonic minor to dominant conversion",
              "quote_excerpt": "it is entirely normal and acceptable to turn minor chords in the diatonic series into dominant 7th chords",
              "page": 95
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "tritone-substitution",
          "name": "Tritone Substitution",
          "summary": "A dominant 7 chord may be replaced by the dominant 7 chord whose root lies a tritone interval away, sharing guide tones (3rd and 7th) with the original.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SubstitutionExpand"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "bIImaj7 as tritone/chromatic sub",
              "quote_excerpt": "a tritone substitution, meaning, a chord is being substituted by another chord with a root a tritone interval away",
              "page": 122
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "backdoor-dominant-substitution",
          "name": "Backdoor Dominant Substitution (bVII7 for V7)",
          "summary": "The V7 chord may be replaced by bVII7 (approaching the tonic by whole step), which shares many notes with an altered V7 and can be expanded to a full backdoor ii-V.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SubstitutionExpand"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 11,
              "topic": "backdoor dominant definition",
              "quote_excerpt": "A backdoor dominant is a dominant 7th chord that substitutes the V7 chord for a bVII7 chord approaching the I chord by a whole step",
              "page": 177
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 11,
              "topic": "backdoor ii-V concept",
              "quote_excerpt": "the Bbmin7-Eb7 is really acting as a backdoor ii-V into the Fmaj7",
              "page": 178
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "diminished-as-rootless-dominant-b9",
          "name": "Diminished 7 as Rootless Dominant b9 Substitute",
          "summary": "A diminished 7 chord may substitute for a dominant 7(b9) chord by functioning as its rootless voicing; all pitch classes are shared except the bass root.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SubstitutionExpand"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 9,
              "topic": "Diminished 7 as rootless dominant b9",
              "quote_excerpt": "You can think of the F#dim7 as a rootless D7(b9)",
              "page": 141
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 9,
              "topic": "Diminished chord as dominant substitute",
              "quote_excerpt": "The F#dim7 chord is a substitute for the dominant VI7 chord",
              "page": 141
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "secondary-dominant-tonicization",
          "name": "Secondary Dominant Tonicization (ii-V Expansion)",
          "summary": "Any diatonic chord may be preceded by its secondary dominant (V7/x) or a full ii-V pair, temporarily tonicizing that chord as a new tonic.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SubstitutionExpand"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "secondary dominant definition",
              "quote_excerpt": "A secondary dominant is when a dominant 7 chord acts as a V chord of a diatonic chord other than the tonic",
              "page": 62
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 5,
              "topic": "ii-V of diatonic chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "it's just as easy to add a ii chord in front of it to create a ii-V chord progression",
              "page": 78
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "hybrid-minor-ii-v-major-resolution",
          "name": "Hybrid ii-V-I (Minor ii-V Resolving to Major I)",
          "summary": "The ii and V chords may be borrowed from the parallel minor key while resolving to the major I chord, creating an expressive surprise.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SubstitutionExpand"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 11,
              "topic": "hybrid ii-V-I definition",
              "quote_excerpt": "A hybrid ii-V-I is when a minor ii-V-i is mixed with a major ii-V-I. The ii and V are borrowed from the minor key, but they resolve to the major I",
              "page": 179
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 11,
              "topic": "hybrid ii-V-I expressive effect",
              "quote_excerpt": "it gives you the sense that it will resolve to a minor chord, but then unexpectedly, it resolves to a major chord",
              "page": 180
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "minimal-change-voice-leading",
          "name": "Minimal-Change Voice Leading (Only Alter 3rds and 7ths)",
          "summary": "When navigating harmonically dense or shifting passages, adjust only the notes that define chord quality \u2014 the 3rds and 7ths \u2014 keeping all other melodic tones constant.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "VoiceMotion"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "minimal note changes between chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "I try to adjust the notes minimally, only to accommodate the changes necessary from one chord to another",
              "page": 129
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "defining notes / chord difference question",
              "quote_excerpt": "What are the defining notes of each chord? In other words, which notes differ from the chord before it",
              "page": 127
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "extensions-over-7th-chords",
          "name": "Extension Layer Permission (9ths, 11ths, 13ths)",
          "summary": "Any 7th chord may be extended with 9th, 11th, or 13th tones; these upper extensions are available as color tones in improvisation.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "ColorToneRequire"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "extensions beyond 7th chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "Utilizing the 9th, 11th and 13th can have a powerful effect",
              "page": 132
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "chromatic-passing-tone",
          "name": "Chromatic Passing Tone Insertion",
          "summary": "Non-diatonic notes may be inserted between two diatonic notes by half-step to connect them; these are labeled chromatic passing tones and do not represent structural chord tones.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "NCTHarmonization"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "chromatic passing tone definition",
              "quote_excerpt": "A chromatic passing tone is a non-diatonic note that connects two diatonic notes in a passage, usually by half steps",
              "page": 130
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 4,
              "topic": "chromatic approach note",
              "quote_excerpt": "the note in question does not represent a basic chord tone, extension or alteration, but is simply chromatically approaching a diatonic note",
              "page": 65
            }
          ]
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "brent-vaartstra:jazz-standards-playbook:modal-harmony-system",
      "name": "Modal Harmony Improvisation System",
      "summary": "This system governs improvisation in modal harmonic contexts \u2014 pieces where chord movement is minimal or static and modes (rather than functional ii-V-I progressions) define the pitch language. Its members are the seven modes of the major scale, used as named pitch collections over sustained key centers. The central traversal rule is that modal arpeggios connect via the nearest chord tone rather than always starting on the root, and adjacent modes link through half- or whole-step motion. The defining modification rule is that guide tones (3rds and 7ths) are not required in modal contexts \u2014 the mode itself is the resource pool, and idiomatic modal playing means using modes as note choices rather than running them as scales. The Miles Davis approach to So What (sparse, space-conscious, phrase-then-expand, Dorian with occasional chromatic approach notes) is modeled as the aesthetic standard for this system.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "ionian",
          "name": "Ionian (Major) \u2014 Mode 1",
          "summary": "Parent scale; W-W-H-W-W-W-H."
        },
        {
          "id": "dorian",
          "name": "Dorian \u2014 Mode 2",
          "summary": "Primary mode for modal jazz; W-H-W-W-W-H-W; scale tone formula 1-2-b3-4-5-6-b7."
        },
        {
          "id": "phrygian",
          "name": "Phrygian \u2014 Mode 3",
          "summary": "H-W-W-W-H-W-W; darkest diatonic mode."
        },
        {
          "id": "lydian",
          "name": "Lydian \u2014 Mode 4",
          "summary": "W-W-W-H-W-W-H; raised 4th gives bright, floating quality."
        },
        {
          "id": "mixolydian",
          "name": "Mixolydian \u2014 Mode 5",
          "summary": "Major scale with b7; formula 1-2-3-4-5-6-b7; primary scale for dominant 7 chords."
        },
        {
          "id": "aeolian",
          "name": "Aeolian (Natural Minor) \u2014 Mode 6",
          "summary": "W-H-W-W-H-W-W; natural minor scale."
        },
        {
          "id": "locrian",
          "name": "Locrian \u2014 Mode 7",
          "summary": "H-W-W-H-W-W-W; built on the leading tone; basis for half-diminished chord."
        },
        {
          "id": "whole-half-diminished",
          "name": "Whole-Half Diminished Scale",
          "summary": "Eight-note scale; W-H-W-H-W-H-W; used over diminished 7 and dominant b9 chords."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "modal-arpeggio-nearest-tone",
          "name": "Modal Arpeggio: Enter on Nearest Chord Tone",
          "summary": "When connecting arpeggios across modal chord changes, begin the next arpeggio on whichever chord tone is closest to the last note played, not necessarily the root.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "VoiceMotion"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "modal arpeggio exercise principle",
              "quote_excerpt": "connected each arpeggio with the nearest chord tone of the proceeding chord",
              "page": 112
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "modal-pattern-in-thirds",
          "name": "Modal Pattern Exercise in 3rds with Half/Whole-Step Mode Links",
          "summary": "Adjacent modes are connected through 3rd-based patterns that transition to the next mode by either a half step or whole step, internalizing mode relationships across the parent scale.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "StringSetTransition"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "modal pattern exercise in 3rds",
              "quote_excerpt": "the pattern moves in 3rds and connects to the next mode in either a half step or a whole step",
              "page": 113
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "space-phrase-expand",
          "name": "Space-and-Expand Phrasing (Miles Davis Model)",
          "summary": "In modal contexts, play a phrase, leave space, then expand upon that phrase \u2014 avoiding continuous note-running in favor of sparse, intentional melodic statements.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "TextureCycle"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "Miles Davis style characteristics",
              "quote_excerpt": "He plays a phrase, leaves some space, and then expands upon that phrase",
              "page": 114
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "guide-tones-omitted-modal",
          "name": "Guide Tone Omission in Modal Contexts",
          "summary": "In modal harmony with minimal chord movement, guide tones (3rds and 7ths) are not required as connective tissue; the mode as pitch collection replaces the guide-tone framework.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "OmissionAllow"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "guide tones omitted for modal tunes",
              "quote_excerpt": "there is no need to identify the guide tones",
              "page": 103
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "scales-as-pitch-collections",
          "name": "Scales as Pitch Collections (Not Linear Drills)",
          "summary": "Modes and scales are to be used as harmonic resource pools \u2014 non-ordered sets of available note choices \u2014 rather than linear sequences to be played up and down.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "ColorToneRequire"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 6,
              "topic": "scales as pitch collections",
              "quote_excerpt": "The best way to think about the use of scales is 'pitch collections'",
              "page": 99
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "modes as note choices, not scales",
              "quote_excerpt": "this doesn't mean you should play the scales like scales. You should just use them as note choices",
              "page": 111
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "chromatic-approach-in-modal",
          "name": "Chromatic Approach Notes in Modal Playing",
          "summary": "Within Dorian or other modal contexts, occasional chromatic approach notes are permitted as expressive exceptions that do not disrupt modal identity.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "NCTHarmonization"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 7,
              "topic": "Miles Davis style characteristics",
              "quote_excerpt": "He generally plays within the Dorian mode, with exceptions of course and occasional chromatic approach notes",
              "page": 114
            }
          ]
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "brent-vaartstra:jazz-standards-playbook:ear-first-acquisition",
      "name": "Ear-First Acquisition System (LIST Process)",
      "summary": "This system describes the procedural method by which a jazz musician should learn any standard. Its members are the four sequential stages of the LIST process (Listen, Internalize, Sing, Transfer), each with defined entry criteria and outputs. Traversal rules specify the mandatory order of progression through the stages \u2014 instrument put away during Listen, undivided attention during Internalize, singing the melody alone as a pass/fail gate before Transfer, and melody learned before chords within the Transfer stage. The modification rules specify enhancements within stages: including vocal versions and learning lyrics to deepen melodic internalization, using Frank Sinatra as the reference source for straight melody, isolating bass notes to train harmonic ear, and using sheet music only as a verification check rather than a primary learning source.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "listen",
          "name": "Listen \u2014 Stage 1",
          "summary": "Instrument put away; deliberate listening to multiple recordings; no playing."
        },
        {
          "id": "internalize",
          "name": "Internalize \u2014 Stage 2",
          "summary": "Undivided, distraction-free attention; treat music like a movie \u2014 full engagement."
        },
        {
          "id": "sing",
          "name": "Sing \u2014 Stage 3",
          "summary": "Sing melody both with recording and alone; pass/fail gate before touching instrument; optionally sing bass notes for harmony."
        },
        {
          "id": "transfer",
          "name": "Transfer \u2014 Stage 4",
          "summary": "Melody first, then chords; find on instrument what can already be sung; chord learning via root identification then quality determination."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "list-sequential-order",
          "name": "LIST Stages Must Be Traversed in Order",
          "summary": "The four stages must be completed in sequence \u2014 Listen before Internalize, Internalize before Sing, Sing before Transfer \u2014 with each stage's output serving as the prerequisite for the next.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "PositionContinuity"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 1,
              "topic": "LIST process overview",
              "quote_excerpt": "Listen / Internalize / Sing / Transfer",
              "page": 15
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 1,
              "topic": "Sing step: proof of internalization",
              "quote_excerpt": "Be sure that you can sing the melody of the standard, both along with the recording and on your own, before you learn it on your instrument",
              "page": 17
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "melody-before-chords-in-transfer",
          "name": "Melody Before Chords Within Transfer Stage",
          "summary": "Within the Transfer stage, learn the melody on the instrument first; only after melody is established move to learning chord progressions by ear.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "PositionContinuity"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 1,
              "topic": "Transfer step: melody then chords",
              "quote_excerpt": "Transfer the melody. Start learning the melody on your instrument",
              "page": 18
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "root-then-quality-chord-identification",
          "name": "Root Identification Before Quality Determination",
          "summary": "When learning chords by ear, first identify the bass note (root), then determine the chord quality; these are two sequential cognitive operations, not simultaneous.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "PositionContinuity"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 1,
              "topic": "Pro tip: bass note identification",
              "quote_excerpt": "if you can identify that the bass player is playing a concert Eb note for the first chord, then all you have to do is figure out what quality it is",
              "page": 18
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "include-vocal-versions",
          "name": "Include Vocal Recordings and Learn Lyrics",
          "summary": "During the Listen stage, always include at least one vocal version; learning lyrics deepens melodic memory, especially for instrumentalists who would otherwise skip this step.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "_pending:aural-enrichment-protocol"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 1,
              "topic": "Pro tip: learn the lyrics",
              "quote_excerpt": "Learning lyrics is extremely helpful for internalizing melodies",
              "page": 16
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "sinatra-reference-for-straight-melody",
          "name": "Use Frank Sinatra (or Original Artist) as Straight Melody Reference",
          "summary": "To establish a reliable, unembellished melody reference, use Frank Sinatra recordings (or the original composer/artist); avoid learning melody from artists known to heavily ornament.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "_pending:aural-enrichment-protocol"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 1,
              "topic": "Pro tip: finding straight melody",
              "quote_excerpt": "Frank Sinatra is your friend. Frank almost always sings melodies straight",
              "page": 17
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "sing-bass-notes-for-harmony",
          "name": "Extend Sing Stage to Bass Notes for Harmonic Training",
          "summary": "To add harmonic ear training, extend the Sing stage by singing the bass line (root motion) of the standard, not just the melody.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "_pending:aural-enrichment-protocol"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 1,
              "topic": "Singing bass notes for harmony",
              "quote_excerpt": "try singing the bass notes. This will help you begin to learn the harmony of the jazz standard",
              "page": 17
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "sheet-music-as-verification-only",
          "name": "Sheet Music as Verification Tool Only",
          "summary": "Sheet music may be used to check what has been learned by ear, but must not be used as the primary learning source; ear comes first, notation confirms.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "OmissionAllow"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 1,
              "topic": "Sheet music proper role",
              "quote_excerpt": "The idea is not to throw away sheet music but to know its place",
              "page": 12
            }
          ]
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "brent-vaartstra:jazz-standards-playbook:improvisation-practice-toolkit",
      "name": "Improvisation Practice Toolkit System",
      "summary": "This system organizes the concrete improvisation practice methods taught across all chapters into a structured toolkit. Its members are the named practice tools: guide tones, melody reference, chord tones/arpeggios, contrafact composition, motif development, transcribed licks, and composed solos. Traversal rules govern how a practitioner sequences or escalates across tools \u2014 beginning with harmonic reduction (guide tones alone, or single-note practice) before adding melodic complexity, scaffolding through the three-chorus melody exercise from straight melody to free improvisation, and transposing all learned material through all 12 keys for full internalization. Modification rules govern how each tool may be altered or combined.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "guide-tones-tool",
          "name": "Guide Tones Practice Tool",
          "summary": "Isolated half- or quarter-note 3rds and 7ths through the full song form; primary harmonic-melodic spine."
        },
        {
          "id": "melody-reference-tool",
          "name": "Melody Reference Tool",
          "summary": "Using the tune's written melody as a guideline during improvisation; scaffolds departure from fully straight to freely improvised."
        },
        {
          "id": "chord-tones-arpeggios-tool",
          "name": "Chord Tones / Arpeggios Tool",
          "summary": "Playing root, 3rd, 5th, and 7th as ordered arpeggios through changes; voice-led to nearest tone at each transition."
        },
        {
          "id": "contrafact-tool",
          "name": "Contrafact Composition Tool",
          "summary": "A new melody composed over existing chord changes; may be simple/singable or bebop-style; must contain thematic repetition and structure."
        },
        {
          "id": "motif-tool",
          "name": "Motif Development Tool",
          "summary": "Short rhythmic or melodic cells repeated and varied throughout a solo to create coherence over complex changes."
        },
        {
          "id": "transcribed-licks-tool",
          "name": "Transcribed Licks Tool",
          "summary": "Licks learned from recordings of great jazz musicians, internalized and transposed through 12 keys."
        },
        {
          "id": "composed-solos-tool",
          "name": "Composed Solos Tool",
          "summary": "Full solos composed at slow pace (written or memorized on instrument) to develop jazz language and phrase construction."
        }
      ],
      "traversal_rules": [
        {
          "id": "three-chorus-melody-escalation",
          "name": "Three-Chorus Melody Escalation",
          "summary": "Practice escalates across three choruses: first chorus plays melody straight, second chorus embellishes it as a guideline, third chorus improvises freely while referencing melodic fragments.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "TextureCycle"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 3,
              "topic": "Three-chorus melody exercise",
              "quote_excerpt": "The first chorus, just play the melody completely straight",
              "page": 49
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "twelve-keys-internalization",
          "name": "All Licks and Patterns Through All 12 Keys",
          "summary": "Every lick, pattern, or harmonic concept must be practiced in all 12 keys to achieve full internalization and strengthen weak key areas.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "SymmetryMovement"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 6,
              "topic": "practicing licks in 12 keys",
              "quote_excerpt": "I always suggest taking things through all 12 keys. It's a powerful practice that not only helps you internalize musical information",
              "page": 101
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "density-reduction-for-complex-passages",
          "name": "Density Reduction for Harmonically Dense Passages",
          "summary": "When facing confusing chord changes, reduce note density radically \u2014 down to a single note over an entire sequence \u2014 to isolate chord-tone hearing before adding complexity.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "DensityFloor"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "playing fewer notes as practice",
              "quote_excerpt": "try playing as few notes as possible over a given chord progression. Sometimes it can even have a profound effect to play just one note over an entire sequence",
              "page": 132
            },
            {
              "chapter_n": 8,
              "topic": "keep it simple over confusing changes",
              "quote_excerpt": "Whenever I come across a section within a piece of music that seems confusing",
              "page": 127
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "quarter-note-solo-rhythmic-reduction",
          "name": "Quarter-Note Solo Exercise (Rhythmic Reduction)",
          "summary": "Practice improvisation using only quarter notes to remove rhythmic complexity, isolating harmonic navigation as the sole focus.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "DensityFloor"
          },
          "references": [
            {
              "chapter_n": 11,
              "topic": "quarter note solo exercise",
              "quote_excerpt": "I stick to a quarter note rhythm in all of my note choices. I take out the rhythmic component from my improvisation and just focus on navigating the changes",
              "page": 184
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "modification_rules": [
        {
          "id": "contrafact-simplicity-rule",
          "name": "Contrafact Singability and Simplicity Requirement",
          "summary": "A student contrafact should be simple enough to sing, should outline chord changes through chord tones, and must express a coherent theme; complexity is secondary to melodic logic.",
          "engine_payload": {
            "kind": "DensityCeiling"
          },
          "references": [
            {
